# Topic Overview
The topic discusses adaptive modulation techniques, specifically QPSK and 16-QAM, in wireless communication systems. It highlights the working principle, performance, and impact of fading conditions on system performance.

# Key Concepts
- Adaptive modulation
- QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)
- 16-QAM (16-Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)
- Rician channel and K-factor
- AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channel
- SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)
- BER (Bit Error Rate)

# Important Points
- Adaptive modulation is used to maximize data throughput based on the SNR values.
- QPSK is selected when 10 dB ≤ SNR < 18 dB for a balance between data rate and reliability.
- 16-QAM is used at high SNR values to maximize data throughput.
- The Rician channel is characterized by the K-factor, which represents the ratio of power in the Line-of-Sight component to the scattered components.
- A higher K-factor results in lower BER across all distances, indicating a strong Line-of-Sight component.

# Formulas (if applicable)
- Transmitted QPSK signal: s(t) = √(2/Ts) [ I cos(2πf_c t) + Q sin(2πf_c t)] (11)
- Alternative representation of QPSK: s(t) = √(Eb/Tb) [ I cos(2πf_c t) + Q sin(2πf_c t)] (9)
- BER performance of QPSK in an AWGN channel: BER = Q(√(2Eb/N₀)) (10)

# Quick Revision Notes
- Adaptive modulation switches between QPSK and 16-QAM based on SNR.
- QPSK represents 2 bits per symbol and is used in moderate channel conditions.
- 16-QAM is a higher-order modulation technique that varies both amplitude and phase for higher data transmission rates.
- The K-factor in the Rician channel affects system performance, with higher K-factors resulting in better performance.
